title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Jane Hobbs, accusing her and Samuel Kid of conspiring to frame Nancy Drew for the murders of Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransberry, and Richard Nigges at the Bluxton House Hotel. Poirot argues that the evidence against Nancy Drew is too convenient and illogical. He notes that the killer left a note at the front desk announcing the murders with the numbers "121, 238, 317," yet supposedly panicked and dropped two keys in front of Mr. Kid. Poirot points out that Nancy Drew had an alibi, having had dinner with Mrs. Louisa Wallace, and that the "panic" displayed by the supposed killer contradicts the calm manner required to leave a note at the desk.

Poirot identifies two major similarities between the hotel murders and a previous incident at the Browns Hotel involving a woman named Miss Jean. Psychologically, in both cases, the victims appeared to deserve death, with the perpetrator acting as an agent of justice. Factually, both crimes provided an excessive amount of information to the police, such as the motive for retribution and specific details like the cufflinks placed in the victims' mouths. 

Poirot reveals that Jane Hobbs targeted him at "The Happy Café" because she learned of his reputation as a meticulous former European police officer with connections to Scotland Yard. He asserts that her terrified demeanor at the café was a calculated performance designed to manipulate him into investigating the case and helping her frame Nancy Drew. Finally, Poirot questions how Jane could have known the specific, unusual detail that the killer would place cufflinks in the victims' mouths unless she was involved in the planning.
